notable.notable_blurb: Invented the cotton gin; pioneered interchangeable parts

institutions.institution_blurb: Adjacent museum preserving the Whitney Armory site established 1798.

education.notes: B.A. 1792, Phi Beta Kappa. Worked as a schoolteacher to pay tuition; entered Yale at 24, graduated at 27.

narrative_blocks.header: Invented the cotton gin during a plantation visit
narrative_blocks.body: Visiting Catherine Littlefield Greene's Mulberry Grove plantation in 1793 as a tutor, he heard the difficulty of separating short-staple cotton from its seeds. He built a working prototype in ten days and a finished machine in six months.

narrative_blocks.header: Cotton gin made the South wealthy, not him
narrative_blocks.body: His 1794 patent was widely pirated by Southern planters, and he spent years in court collecting only a fraction of his awarded damages. The cotton gin made cotton economically viable again and revitalized Southern slavery — a consequence that haunted Whitney, who was a Northern abolitionist by sympathy.

narrative_blocks.header: Pioneered American mass production with a musket contract
narrative_blocks.body: After his patent disaster he took a 1798 federal contract for ten thousand muskets and built a factory at Whitneyville, Connecticut using interchangeable parts. In 1801 he demonstrated to John Adams and Thomas Jefferson by picking parts at random from boxes and assembling working muskets on the spot.

narrative_blocks.header: Worked his way to Yale at twenty-four
narrative_blocks.body: He earned his tuition teaching school in Westborough and graduated Yale Phi Beta Kappa in 1792, four years older than his classmates. He had originally planned to study law but took a tutoring job in Georgia after graduation — the trip that would lead him to Catherine Greene's plantation.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Jonathan Edwards's granddaughter at fifty-one
narrative_blocks.body: In 1817 he married Henrietta Frances Edwards, granddaughter of the theologian Jonathan Edwards, who was twenty-eight years his junior. They had four children before his death from prostate cancer in January 1825.

orbit_connections.name: Samuel F.B. Morse
orbit_connections.relationship: Painter of Eli Whitney's portrait (1822) — five years after Whitney's marriage to Henrietta Frances Edwards (HD2046), seven years before Morse's telegraph patent
orbit_connections.notes:
Samuel Finley Breese Morse (1791-1872) painted the definitive portrait of Eli Whitney in 1822, three years before Whitney's death. Oil on canvas, 35 7/8 × 27 3/4 in. Yale Art Gallery accession 1827.1 — accession number 1827.1 suggests this is among the very first formally catalogued works in the Yale Art Gallery collection. Yale Art Gallery: https://artgallery.yale.edu/collections/objects/31 

GIFT: Donated to Yale by George Hoadley (B.A. 1801) — the 4th Mayor of New Haven, husband of Mary Ann Woolsey Hoadley (HD2075, a Hooker descendant), and the man whose Eagle Bank presidency had nearly bankrupted Yale in 1825. The gift came in 1827 — two years after Whitney's death, two years after the bank collapse. Whether it was redemptive gesture or simply the natural action of New Haven's social elite, the Hooker family network is woven through every thread: Whitney married a Hooker descendant (HD2046); Hoadley married a Hooker descendant (HD2075); Hoadley donated the portrait to Yale, whose presidency was held by Henrietta's first cousin Timothy Dwight IV (H00914). 

Morse also taught at NYU; Theodore William Dwight (HD1989), Columbia Law School founder, studied under Morse — connecting the Hooker tree to Morse through two separate branches. (Yale Art Gallery; Wikipedia; NYC Digital Collections)

narrative_blocks.header: Married Eli Whitney
narrative_blocks.detail: The marriage united two of New England's most prominent family networks: the Edwards-Hooker theological dynasty and the Whitney manufacturing empire. Whitney was already famous — his cotton gin had been patented in 1794, his musket contracts had made him wealthy, and he had pioneered interchangeable parts manufacturing. He was also in failing health; he died eight years after the wedding. Henrietta survived him by 45 years, dying ~1870 — long enough to witness the Emancipation Proclamation and the end of the war the cotton gin had helped make economically intractable. His portrait had been painted by Samuel F.B. Morse in 1822 — five years after the wedding, seven years before Morse's telegraph. (Wikipedia; FindAGrave)
narrative_blocks.body: Henrietta Frances Edwards married Eli Whitney on January 6, 1817, when she was 30 and he was 51. Whitney's biographer noted the marriage "further tied him to Connecticut's ruling elite" — she was a granddaughter of Jonathan Edwards, daughter of Connecticut's Democratic Party chief, and first cousin of Yale's Federalist president Timothy Dwight IV.

narrative_blocks.header: She outlived the cotton gin’s inventor by decades
narrative_blocks.body: As Eli Whitney’s widow she lived forty-five more years — long enough to see the Emancipation Proclamation and the end of the Civil War, the full reckoning of what her husband’s machine had set loose on the country.

bio.bio_blurb: Wife of cotton-gin inventor Eli Whitney

notable.notable_blurb: Industrialist; founded the New Haven Water Company

cross_connections.display_label: armed the Union as Bradley lit it from Meriden
supplied Union arms while Whitney's armory did too

institutions.institution_blurb: Adjacent museum preserving the Whitney Armory site established 1798.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died when he was four years old
narrative_blocks.body: When the cotton gin inventor died in January 1825, his son was four. He was raised by his mother Henrietta Edwards Whitney in the Edwards family's intellectual tradition and took control of the family arms factory in his early twenties.

narrative_blocks.header: Built Whitneyville into a Civil War arsenal
narrative_blocks.body: He took over the Whitney Arms Company in 1842 and ran it for the next forty-six years. Under his management the company supplied tens of thousands of rifled muskets to the Union Army during the Civil War.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded the New Haven Water Company in 1860
narrative_blocks.body: He constructed the dam that created Lake Whitney in 1860 and founded the New Haven Water Company to supply the growing city. The company became New Haven's primary water utility and remained in family management for two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Sold Whitney Arms to Winchester in 1888
narrative_blocks.body: In 1888 he sold the firearms business — the firm his father founded under the 1798 musket contract — to Winchester Repeating Arms. The sale ended ninety years of Whitney firearms manufacturing in Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Followed Edwards tradition to Princeton in 1841
narrative_blocks.body: He attended Princeton University, following the path of his maternal great-grandfather Jonathan Edwards, who had served briefly as the college's president. He graduated Princeton in 1841 at age twenty.

career.notes: Modernized and expanded the family arms factory; supplied Civil War muskets to Union Army; sold to Winchester Repeating Arms in 1888, ending 90 years of Whitney firearms manufacturing.
Constructed the dam creating Lake Whitney in 1860 and founded the company that became New Haven's primary water utility.

orbit_connections.name: Eli Whitney Debevoise
orbit_connections.relationship: Great-great-grandson of Eli Whitney and Henrietta Frances Edwards (HD2046), through Eli Whitney IV (HD2089) → Anne Farnam Whitney → Eli Whitney Debevoise
orbit_connections.notes: Eli Whitney Debevoise (Dec 14, 1899 – Jun 30, 1990). Yale 1921, Harvard Law 1925. Co-founded law firm Debevoise & Plimpton (1931, with $2,366 in their account). Represented Alger Hiss at his perjury trials. WWII: Chairman of the Alien Enemy Hearing Board. Founder of the International Commission of Jurists. Legal counsel to Ford Motor Company as it became a public corporation. His grandson Eli Whitney Debevoise II (b. 1953, Yale 1974, Harvard Law 1977) was US Executive Director of the World Bank 2007-2009. (Wikipedia; FindAGrave)

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, the railroad and canal builder

institutions.institution_blurb: Adjacent museum preserving the Whitney Armory site established 1798.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1869 like his cotton-gin grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale College in 1869, his grandfather Eli Whitney had graduated Yale Phi Beta Kappa in 1792. He continued the family tradition of New Haven engineering and civic leadership.

narrative_blocks.header: Succeeded his father as Water Company president
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited his father's role at the New Haven Water Company and ran it as president for nearly four decades. The position made him a central figure in turn-of-the-century New Haven civic life.

narrative_blocks.header: Seven daughters closed the male Whitney line
narrative_blocks.body: He and Sarah Sheffield Farnam had seven daughters but no surviving sons, ending the male line of Whitney inventors and arms makers. Through his daughter Anne the Whitney name passed into the Debevoise family of New York lawyers.

orbit_connections.name: Bishop Chauncey Bunce Brewster
orbit_connections.notes: Chauncey Bunce Brewster (Sep 5, 1848 – Apr 9, 1941). 5th Bishop of the Episcopal Diocese of Connecticut (1897-1928). Yale 1868, Phi Beta Kappa, Skull and Bones. Berkeley Divinity School 1872. Descendant of Mayflower passenger William Brewster. Attended Hopkins Grammar School. His wife Susan and their infant son Eli Whitney Brewster both died in May 1885. Brewster Hall at Berkeley Divinity School named in his honor. Author: The Key of Life (1894), Aspects of Revelation (1901). Died at age 92. (Wikipedia; FindAGrave) INSTITUTION AFFILIATIONS: Yale 1868 (Phi Beta Kappa, Skull and Bones); Berkeley Divinity School 1872. Doctor of Divinity: Trinity College (1897), Yale (1898), Wesleyan University (1903). Hopkins Grammar School (preparatory). Brewster Hall at Berkeley Divinity School named in his honor 1940.

INST108.name: Eli Whitney Museum and Workshop
INST108.notes: Located at 915 Whitney Avenue, Hamden CT 06517 — adjacent to the Whitney Armory site originally established by Eli Whitney (X00655) in 1798 as the first private firearms manufactory in the United States. Displays span the 1798-1888 history of the armory, the cotton gin, the construction of Lake Whitney (LM060), the New Haven Water Company, and the Whitney family. The museum serves as a learning center for science, technology, and industrial history. Phone: 203-777-1833.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
